content.body: Magnetism is a fundamental force of nature. Magnets attract certain metals (iron, nickel, cobalt) and can attract or repel other magnets. The Earth itself is a giant magnet, which is why compasses work.

Poles of a magnet:
• North pole (N) and South pole (S)
• LIKE poles repel: N↔N, S↔S
• UNLIKE poles attract: N↔S

Every magnet has BOTH poles — you cannot isolate a single pole.
If you cut a magnet, each piece becomes a complete magnet with N and S poles.

content.title: Magnetic Field Lines
content.body:
Magnetic field lines show the direction and strength of a magnetic field:
• Lines go from North pole to South pole outside the magnet
• Where lines are CLOSER TOGETHER: field is STRONGER
• Field lines never cross
• A compass needle aligns with field lines

The Earth's geographic North Pole is actually a magnetic SOUTH pole (attracts the N end of a compass).

Induced Magnetism:
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt) can be temporarily or permanently magnetised.

Iron: magnetised easily but loses magnetism quickly (soft magnetic material — good for electromagnets)
Steel: harder to magnetise but retains magnetism (hard magnetic material — good for permanent magnets)

Methods of magnetisation: stroking with a magnet, placing in a solenoid with DC current, heating and cooling in a magnetic field

content.title: Electromagnets
content.body:
An electromagnet is made by coiling wire around iron and passing electric current through it.

Strength of electromagnet increased by:
1. Increasing current through coil
2. Increasing number of turns of wire
3. Using an iron core (not air core)
4. Using soft iron (loses magnetism quickly when current stops)

Applications: electric bells, relays, electric motors, cranes in scrapyards, MRI scanners

questions.question: Like magnetic poles:
questions.answer: Repel each other
questions.explanation: Like poles (N-N or S-S) repel. Unlike poles (N-S) attract.

questions.question: If you cut a bar magnet in half, you get:
options: One N and one S pole separately
Two complete magnets each with N and S
questions.answer: Two complete magnets each with N and S
questions.explanation: You cannot isolate a magnetic pole. Each piece becomes a complete magnet with both N and S poles.

questions.question: Magnetic field lines are closest together where the field is:
questions.answer: Strongest
questions.explanation: Closer field lines indicate a stronger magnetic field — like near the poles of a magnet.

questions.question: Iron is used for electromagnet cores (not steel) because:
options: Iron loses magnetism quickly when current stops
questions.answer: Iron loses magnetism quickly when current stops
questions.explanation: Soft iron magnetises easily and demagnetises quickly — ideal for electromagnets that need to switch on and off.

questions.question: How can you increase the strength of an electromagnet?
options: Use an iron core and increase current
questions.answer: Use an iron core and increase current
questions.explanation: More current, more turns, and a soft iron core all increase electromagnet strength.

questions.question: A compass needle points North because:
options: The Earth's geographic North is a magnetic North
Earth's geographic North is a magnetic South pole
questions.answer: Earth's geographic North is a magnetic South pole
questions.explanation: The geographic North Pole is actually a magnetic South pole — it attracts the North pole of a compass needle.

questions.question: Which material is NOT attracted to a magnet?
questions.answer: Copper
questions.explanation: Only ferromagnetic materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) are attracted to magnets. Copper is non-magnetic.

questions.question: Steel is used for permanent magnets because:
options: It retains magnetism well after being magnetised
questions.answer: It retains magnetism well after being magnetised
questions.explanation: Hard magnetic materials like steel retain magnetism — good for permanent magnets.

questions.question: Magnetic field lines travel from:
options: S to N inside the magnet only
N to S outside the magnet
questions.answer: N to S outside the magnet
questions.explanation: Outside the magnet, field lines run from the North pole to the South pole.

questions.question: An MRI scanner in a hospital uses:
questions.answer: Strong electromagnetic fields
questions.explanation: MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) uses powerful electromagnets to create images of the inside of the body.

content.body: Thermal energy (heat) is the internal energy of a substance — the total kinetic energy of all its particles. Heat naturally flows from hotter regions to cooler regions until thermal equilibrium is reached. There are three mechanisms of heat transfer.

CONDUCTION:
• Transfer of heat through a solid without movement of the material
• Particle vibrations pass energy to neighbouring particles
• Best conductors: metals (copper, silver, aluminium)
• Poor conductors (insulators): wood, plastic, rubber, air, water, wool
• Used in: cooking pans, heat sinks in computers

CONVECTION:
• Transfer of heat through fluids (liquids and gases) by movement of the fluid itself
• Hot fluid rises (less dense), cool fluid sinks (more dense) → convection current
• Cannot occur in solids
• Examples: central heating, sea breezes, hot air balloons, ocean currents

RADIATION:
• Transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves (infrared radiation)
• Does NOT need a medium — works in vacuum (space)
• All objects emit and absorb radiation
• Dark/dull/rough surfaces: better emitters AND absorbers
• Light/shiny/smooth surfaces: poor emitters, good reflectors

content.title: Applications of Heat Transfer
content.body:
Thermos flask (vacuum flask) reduces all three:
• Vacuum between walls: no conduction or convection
• Silvered walls: reflect radiation back in

House insulation:
• Loft insulation (fibreglass): reduces conduction and convection
• Double glazing: air gap reduces conduction
• Cavity wall insulation: reduces convection currents

Cooking a radiator black: better emitter of heat into the room

questions.question: Heat transfer through a solid without particle movement is:
questions.answer: Conduction
questions.explanation: Conduction transfers heat through solids via vibrations passed between neighbouring particles.

questions.question: Convection occurs in:
questions.answer: Fluids (liquids and gases) only
questions.explanation: Convection requires fluid flow (bulk movement of particles), so it occurs in liquids and gases only.

questions.question: Which does not need a medium for heat transfer?
questions.answer: Radiation
questions.explanation: Radiation travels as electromagnetic waves and can cross a vacuum — this is how the Sun heats the Earth.

questions.question: Why does hot air rise?
options: Air is always hot at the top
Hot air is less dense so it is pushed up by cooler denser air
questions.answer: Hot air is less dense so it is pushed up by cooler denser air
questions.explanation: Heating makes air molecules move faster and spread out — less dense. The cooler, denser air sinks and pushes the hot air up, creating convection currents.

questions.question: A thermos flask has silvered walls to:
options: Reflect radiation and reduce heat transfer
questions.answer: Reflect radiation and reduce heat transfer
questions.explanation: Silvered (shiny) surfaces are poor emitters and reflect radiation, reducing heat transfer by radiation.

questions.question: Which surface emits the most thermal radiation?
questions.answer: Black rough surface
questions.explanation: Dark, rough, dull surfaces are the best emitters (and absorbers) of thermal radiation.

questions.question: Metals are good thermal conductors because:
questions.answer: Free electrons transfer energy quickly
questions.explanation: Metals have free (delocalized) electrons that move throughout the structure, rapidly transferring thermal energy.

questions.question: In a vacuum flask, the vacuum between walls prevents:
questions.answer: Conduction and convection
questions.explanation: A vacuum has no particles, so conduction (needs particle contact) and convection (needs fluid movement) cannot occur.

questions.question: Sea breezes occur because:
options: Land heats up faster than sea, air rises over land, cooler sea air moves in
The wind always blows from sea to land
questions.answer: Land heats up faster than sea, air rises over land, cooler sea air moves in
questions.explanation: Land warms faster than sea. Hot air over land rises (convection), and cooler air from the sea flows in to replace it — creating a sea breeze.

questions.question: Loft insulation in houses works by:
options: Trapping air and reducing convection and conduction through the roof
questions.answer: Trapping air and reducing convection and conduction through the roof
questions.explanation: Fibreglass loft insulation traps air, which is a poor conductor. This reduces heat transfer by conduction and prevents convection through the ceiling.
